does: This paper introduces OTTER, a black-box red-teaming system that generates jailbreak prompts by decoupling surface-level toxicity from adversarial intent through token-level optimization. The core contribution is demonstrating that production LLM safety filters relying primarily on toxicity classifiers can be bypassed by replacing as few as five tokens in harmful requests, without requiring white-box access or parameter knowledge. Evaluating OTTER on 457 AdvBench prompts across GPT models, the authors show attack success rates increase from 7.0% baseline to 84.0%, revealing a fundamental brittleness in toxicity-based defenses. The paper additionally provides quantitative analysis characterizing the toxicity-bypass relationship and category-specific breakdowns of vulnerabilities, offering empirical evidence that harmful intent and toxic surface wording can be substantially decoupled through practical rewriting strategies.
connects: This work extends prior research on adversarial attacks against LLMs and jailbreaking techniques, building on the recognition that instruction-following models can be manipulated to produce harmful outputs despite safety training. It connects to earlier token-level optimization methods for adversarial attacks while advancing beyond those approaches by operating as a practical black-box system requiring only API access, unlike gradient-based white-box attacks. The paper responds directly to industry reliance on toxicity classifiers as primary defenses, a common assumption in production deployments documented in earlier safety research. It also builds on AdvBench as a benchmark for measuring jailbreak effectiveness and contributes to growing evidence that safety mechanisms relying on surface-level signals are insufficient, extending findings from prior work on prompt injection and instruction override vulnerabilities.
enables: This research enables more robust safety filter design by identifying specific vulnerabilities in toxicity-based approaches and providing actionable hardening recommendations for production deployments. It establishes toxicity-bypass as a distinct attack surface that future defenses must account for, potentially motivating research into deeper semantic understanding of intent rather than surface-level filtering. The quantitative analysis of the toxicity-harm relationship provides a foundation for developing more sophisticated classifiers and multi-layered defense strategies. Additionally, OTTER's practical black-box approach serves as a red-teaming tool for security audits, enabling organizations to stress-test their filters systematically. The work points toward future directions exploring combinations of detection mechanisms, adversarial training on obfuscated prompts, and semantic-level safety mechanisms that cannot be bypassed through simple token substitution.
